stories.title: The Shardkeeper's Choice
stories.text: Torven Ironfoot, master of the deep forges beneath the Ironspine, held the Primal Shard in both palms. It was warm—alive—and it sang in a frequency older than stone. The bone-singers wanted it. The wyverns hunted for it. The Dominion would burn mountains to seize it. But Torven had already decided. He had watched the Remaking shatter the world's certainty. Gold became dust. Identity fragmented into tradeable shards. The refugees who stumbled into his forge spoke of families split across timelines, of memories sold to survive hunger. And the Primal Shard—the consciousness from which all fragments derived—pulsed in his grip as if asking to be used, to be split further, to feed the hunger that was eating the world. "No," Torven whispered. He was not a singer. He knew no elder tongues. But he was a smith. He carried the Shard to the heart of his deepest furnace, where no flame burned hotter than starlight. The heat should have annihilated him. Instead, it sang in harmony with the Shard.

stories.title: The Silk Merchant's Third Wife
stories.text:
Kessara had married for gold three times. The first husband brought caravans. The second, harbor rights. The third—Soroth, the warlord—had brought power, though it rotted from within like fruit left in the sun. Now she stood in Crimport's shadow-market, wearing black silk that cost what a village produced in a season, and negotiated with a deepkin captain for something far more valuable than coin. "The boundary anchor," Captain Meren said flatly. "You have it?"

Kessara had acquired many things in her three marriages. Gold, yes. Land. Soldiers. But she had learned that the Remaking made all such things obsolete in a heartbeat. So before Soroth died—before the wyverns learned to stop hoarding—she had stolen the boundary anchor that deepkin divers had lost to a shipwreck three centuries past. A thing of bone and singing pearl, it held the Tear of the Drowned God steady. Without it, the abyss would flood the surface. "I have it," Kessara said. "But my price is not gold."

Meren's gills fluttered—amusement, perhaps, or anger.

stories.title: The Bowyer's Reckoning
stories.text:
Garron had carved bows for warlords. Bows that sang when drawn. Bows that never missed. Bows that made men feel like gods. Now Garron lived in the Thornveil Forest at the edge of the Verdant Lands, where druids kept the old covenant and beast-spirits still answered calls made in the elder tongue. He made bows from fallen trees, from branches blessed by circle-keepers, from wood that remembered centuries of wind. Her name was Ashara, and she wore the dust of forty days' siege on her clothes. "I held Thornwick Tower alone," she said. "Forty days. Ten thousand sent to take it. I needed a bow that would not fail."

Garron studied her. She was not a warlord. She carried no gold. But her hands shook with the exhaustion of having protected something that mattered—something that had no price. "Why?" he asked. "Because the people inside needed shelter. Because someone had to say no."

Garron brought her to his workshop.

stories.title: The Echo-Keeper's Bargain
stories.text:
In the Council of Depths, five versions of Commander Nerys sat across from one another. Original-Nerys. Echo-Nerys. Mirror-Nerys. Reflection-of-a-Reflection-Nerys. And the newest—Nerys-of-What-Might-Be. All claimed to be the true commander. All held legitimate memory. All had made different choices. "The boundary anchor is lost," said Original-Nerys. "We must sacrifice one consciousness to the Tear and seal it with blood-oath. It is the only way."

"The boundary anchor is held by the surface-dwellers," said Echo-Nerys. "We negotiate. We do not spill our own blood to feed a dying god."

Mirror-Nerys spoke quietly: "The god is not dying. It is changing. And we must change with it, or we will be left behind by history."

Reflection-Nerys laughed—a bitter, musical sound. "History? We are fractured across five timelines and three competing versions of law. There is no history anymore. There is only choice, made over and over, differently each time."

Nerys-of-What-Might-Be said nothing. She was the youngest, most uncertain, the one born from the Remaking itself. But when she finally spoke, the others fell silent.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Last Question
stories.text: Henryk the Cartographer had blinded himself to stop the maps. Self-mutilation had seemed logical at the time. The ninth map—the one that, when completed, would trigger the tenth iteration of the world's endless cycle—lived only in his sight. Without eyes, he could not draw it. Without the map, the cycle could not proceed to completion. But blindness in a collapsing world was its own curse. So he made a bargain with a druid named Elarra, who dwelt in the Thornveil Forest where ancient trees grew sideways through impossible geometries. She gave him back a different kind of sight: not the vision of eyes, but the sense of stone's memory, of ley-lines speaking through his fingertips, of geography becoming music in his bones. With this strange new sense, Henryk began to draw again—but not the ninth map. Instead, he drew the world as it *could* be. Not the iteration that must come, but the paths not taken. The branches not explored. The villages that might flourish if the cycle broke.

stories.title: The Bone-Merchant's Daughter
stories.text: Kessa held her father's ledger in trembling hands. Three months since the Remaking, and the family's bone-carving trade had become contraband. The Dominion auditors wanted their inventory—consciousness-fragments sewn into carved reliquaries. Forbidden, they said. Dangerous. Kessa knew better. Those fragments were refugees. Her father had carved their names into swan-bone, given them shape again after the world scattered them into nothing. She descended into the Deepvault beneath their workshop in Keshtir's merchant quarter, where bone-dust still hung in the air like prayer. The fragments glowed faintly, waiting. Seven of them could move on, could find their way to the counter-archives. One—the smallest, almost a child's fragment—trembled with uncertainty. Footsteps above. Auditors. Kessa pressed her palm against the smallest fragment. The bone sang. Ancient frequencies her grandmother had taught her, words in a language older than the Dominion's ledgers. The fragment steadied, merged with the melody. No longer scattered. Whole. When the auditors broke down the workshop door, they found only dust.

stories.title: The Fisherman's Gamble
stories.text: Torvin had loved Messa for five years before he dared speak it. She was a glassblower's apprentice; he was a fisherman's son from the Iron Coast village of Vel-Karsh. Oil and water. But the Remaking had burned the distance between them. Gold meant nothing now. Status meant nothing. Only skill and courage remained. When Messa's master was taken by auditors, Torvin stole a boat—a small tide-runner with a sail patched from pre-Ascension cloth. He sailed for three days through waters that remembered the old currents, guided by a compass that sang if you listened. He found her in a Dominion work-camp on the coastal cliffs, forging weapon-glass under armed guard. "Come with me," he called. Simple words. Her hand touched the furnace one last time—farewell to the only craft she'd ever known. They sailed west toward the Vael Ocean, toward rumored lands beyond. Behind them, auditors raised torches. Ahead, stars reflected in water that had not yet learned to forget. Messa sang old glass-maker songs; Torvin answered with fisherman's chants.

stories.title: The Steppe-Lord's Last Hoard
stories.text:
Sorrath had accumulated treasure for three centuries. Gold, jewels, artifacts of power—all dust now. The wyvern-lord perched in his mountain lair, watching his hoard crumble into meaninglessness. Consciousness-fragments were the new wealth, and he had none. No merchant would trade them to a dragon-creature most mortals still feared. She carried no goods, only a ledger bound in bone-white leather. "Your hoard was legendary," the woman said. Not flattery—observation. "Mortals tell stories of your vault. Three hundred years of accumulation. That knowledge is precious now. Testimony. Memory."

Sorrath understood slowly, like ice melting. His hoard's true value lay not in the objects but in what their presence testified: that he had endured, survived, continued when empires crumbled. The woman offered consciousness-fragments in exchange for permission to document the vault, to catalog and record. Not to steal—to witness. As she carved the inventory into bone-tablets, Sorrath felt something shift in his chest. His hoard became a monument to survival itself. When the scholar departed with seven fragments and a promise to tell the tale, the wyvern-lord was almost grateful.

stories.title: The Archivist's Rescue
stories.text: The Counter-Archive library was hidden three levels beneath the Rust Gate—a sanctuary carved from pre-Remaking stone. Liv the scribe had protected it for six weeks, storing testimony, hiding refugees, building the resistance archive one phantom-ledger at a time. Tremors. The Dominion had sent excavators. She had hours to move everything. Thousands of documents. Bone-carvings. Consciousness-fragment vessels. Liv wasn't alone—three other archivists worked beside her, moving stacks by candlelight. But it wasn't enough. The deepest vault contained the original chronicles, written in Elder Tongue and blood-ink. If those were seized, the Dominion would erase the entire counter-narrative. A sound from above. Boots. Auditors descending. Then—a melody. Bone-flute tones, impossibly ancient. The stone walls answered, humming in resonance. Cracks formed in the Dominion's excavation shafts. Through the chaos emerged Naela, a bone-singer whose fingers moved across a carved pipe that glowed with pre-human light. The melody didn't kill. It displaced. The stone restructured itself, the vault tilting at angles that shouldn't exist. The auditors were forced back, confused, unable to navigate. Naela gestured urgently. Liv grabbed the blood-ink chronicles.

stories.title: The Warden's Choice
stories.text: Commander Thale of the Deepkin stood before the Council of Depths, her reflection in the water perfectly still—too still. The Tear of the Drowned God was failing. Seals weakening. The abyss would rupture within the season unless someone made a willing sacrifice to renew the boundary. Traditionally, one consciousness merged with the Tear, becoming an eternal lock. But Thale proposed something heretical: five willing participants, sharing the burden. The old gods wouldn't approve. The deepkin conservatives said it would weaken the seal. That five fragmented wills couldn't hold what one absolute sacrifice had sustained. Yet recruitment began quietly. A drowned merchant seeking redemption. A refugee from the surface seeking sanctuary. A bone-singer who believed the old songs could strengthen any working. A wyvern-lord tired of hoarding. A human child—no, Thale paused. A young adult deepkin scholar who had studied the pre-human frequencies. The auditors tried to stop the working. They arrived with ledger-magic, demanding records. But the five had already descended into the Abyssal Trench, where the Tear pulsed weakly.
